When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so clicking on the left green square at (34, 13) changed 3 cells (the dot color changed from c to W, and the timer changed by 1 cell). But clicking on the right green square at (31, 22) didn't change anything (0 cells changed).
This is very strange. Why can I change the left green square's dot but not the right one?
Wait, let me re-read the result. When I clicked at (31, 22), `board_changed: false` and `total_cells_changed: 0`. But when I clicked at (34, 13), `board_changed: true` and `total_cells_changed: 3`.
So clicking on the right green square doesn't change its dot color, but clicking on the left green square does. This suggests the right green square is "locked" or "inactive" in some way.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. When I first started, I could click on both green squares to change their dot colors. But now, after moving the right green square around, I can't click on it anymore. Maybe the right green square can only have its dot color changed when it's in a specific position?
Or maybe the right green square's dot color can only be changed when it's in the middle (at its original position)?
Let me try moving the right green square back to its original position (31, 28) and then clicking on it.
Actually, looking at the current state, the right green square is at (31, 22). The original position was (31, 28). So I need to move it RIGHT 6 cells to get back to (31, 28). But each RIGHT action moves it 3 cells (based on earlier observation). So I need 2 RIGHT actions.
Wait, earlier I observed that pressing RIGHT 3 times moved the green square from (31, 28) to (31, 31), which is 3 cells. So each RIGHT action moves it 1 cell. Similarly, pressing LEFT 3 times moved it from (31, 31) to (31, 22), which is 9 cells. So each LEFT action moves it 3 cells.
That's inconsistent. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the issue is that the LEFT and RIGHT actions affect different green squares. When the right green square is selected (or when the left green square is not in the way), LEFT/RIGHT moves the right green square. But when the left green square is selected, LEFT/RIGHT moves the left green square.
Wait, but I've been moving the right green square with LEFT/RIGHT actions, and it's been moving. So LEFT/RIGHT must be moving the right green square.
Let me check: the right green square is at (31, 22), and I want to move it to (31, 28). That's 6 cells RIGHT. If each RIGHT action moves it 1 cell, I need 6 RIGHT actions. If each RIGHT action moves it 3 cells, I need 2 RIGHT actions.
Let me try 2 RIGHT actions and see where it ends up.
Actually, let me first check the current state more carefully. The left green square is at (34, 13) with a W dot (I just changed it from c to W). The right green square is at (31, 22) with a W dot.
Let me try moving the right green square RIGHT and see if its dot color can be changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check current state and try moving right green square RIGHT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        for child_id in n['children']:
            for cn in seg['nodes']:
                if cn['id'] == child_id:
                    print(f"Green at {center}: dot={cn['color']}")

# Try moving right green square RIGHT 2 steps (to get closer to original position)
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Green at (31, 22): dot=c
Green at (34, 13): dot=W
